title: Aussies ‘are poorer’: RBA boss’ blunt admission as rates hiked for third time
full_text: Other Editions 06 May 2026 Business Aussies ‘are poorer’: RBA boss’ blunt admission as rates hiked for third time theaustralian.com.au Duncan Evans 5 May 2026 07:07 GMT 1374 words English AUSTOL © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. Reserve Bank governor Michele Bullock has bluntly admitted ‘Australians are poorer’ and warned government spending splurges were making it harder to tame out-of-control inflation as interest rates were hiked for the third consecutive time this year. The RBA increased the cash rate by 0.25 percentage points to 4.35 per cent on Tuesday, hammering Aussie households as the central bank moves to strangle surging inflation. In blunt language, Ms Bullock said Australians were now “poorer” in the fallout from the Iran War energy shock, which has immediately degraded incomes and triggered a sharp spike in price pressures across the economy. “Australians are poorer because of this shock to oil prices. We are poorer and there is no way out of that,” she said. She also warned of “anaemic” economic growth of 1.3 per cent in 2026 and growing recession risks. “It’s not a great outcome for Australia. We’ve had a shock that has lowered our real incomes. We are all poorer. That’s what this has done, this war on the other side of the world,” she said. The rates lift, from 4.1 per cent, has been described as a “devastating blow” and marks the third interest rate increase so far this year - wiping out the three cuts the RBA delivered across 2025. The RBA also warned further rate hikes could be on the cards for the rest of this year, saying it will ‘do what it considers necessary’. Aussies with an average home loan of $736,000 will now shell out an extra $2657 a year compared to January before the RBA started raising rates, data from consumer finance company Finder has revealed. Average monthly payments will now jump from $4186 to $4419 with an average home loan rate moving from 5.51 per cent in January to 6.01 per cent now. Compare the Market, meanwhile, says households with a $500,000 loan size will now pay an extra $320 a month. Finder home loans expert Richard Whitten said the hike was a ‘devastating blow’ for homeowners already doing it tough – with 39 per cent of Aussies struggling to pay their mortgage in April. “Being back at 4.35 per cent cash rate will feel like a big backward step for those with a mortgage,” he said. “While this was the peak two years ago, the cost of living has continued to climb since then, meaning the same rate now carries a much heavier weight.”Australia's Cash Rate 2022 Tuesday’s near unanimous move by the RBA was widely expected by economists following a brutal jump in annual headline inflation recorded last week in the fallout from the Iran War. The ABS reported inflation hitting 4.6 per cent in the year through to March, up from 3.7 per cent in February and well above the RBA’s target band of 2-3 per cent. Ms Bullock warned - in the back drop of next week’s expected federal budget cash splash - governments giving money to households will make it harder to dampen demand and control inflation. “The extent to which the government makes up the shortfalls for households by giving them more money, it makes it harder to dampen demand,” she said, adding the Board was working to slow demand. “If we are increasing interest rates, what we are trying to do is slow growth in demand and that hits the private sector – typically consumption, investment,” she said. “To the extent that the government is demanding goods and services of the economy – so in the variety of ways that they do, whether it’s direct expenditure or giving money to households to spend on goods and services in the private sector, that adds to demand.” In giving its reason for the hike, the RBA credited “developments in the Middle East” and higher fuel prices for accelerating already stubborn inflation pressures. The Board voted 8-1 to lift the rate, with one member voting to leave the cash rate unchanged. Ms Bullock said Tuesday’s increase would not stop price rises over the coming six months, but was designed instead to temper “inflation expectations”. “This interest rate rise - we know it will not do anything for inflation in the next six months - we know those prices are coming through,” she said. Deloitte Access Economics partner Stephen Smith warned rates could rise to “levels not seen for around 15 years” and urged for a halt on the government’s spending spree. “The policy outlook has shifted materially in a short period. Avoiding an extended period of restrictive interest rates requires greater alignment between fiscal and monetary settings,” he said. “Next week’s federal budget will be critical. The government will need to demonstrate a genuine commitment to fiscal discipline and structural reform, rather than relying on broadbased, short-term cost-of-living measures that may provide temporary relief while adding to medium-term inflation persistence. “Without reforms that lift productivity and expand supply-side capacity, an economy that was already operating near capacity before the Middle East conflict is likely to remain constrained. “In that environment, inflationary pressures will be more difficult to contain, and the risk of further monetary tightening will remain elevated.”Was it the right decision to hike interest rates for a third time? Multiple experts now warn the government must cut spending to reduce price pressures. “The only solution is to cut government spending,” QUT professor Noel Whittaker said. UNSW’s Evgenia Dechter urged the government to “exercise spending restraint” and keep fiscal policy aligned with monetary policy and disinflation. AMP chief economist Shane Oliver called for a “cut” to government spending. “The surge in oil prices is global in nature but underlying inflation of 3.3 per cent is way above target and mostly homegrown due to capacity constraints and poor productivity,” he said. “So the solution to getting underlying inflation back down sustainably is to cut back government spending to free up capacity in the economy and cut regulation and reform the tax system to boost productivity.” Treasurer Jim Chalmers, responding to the hike, defended his spending policies in light of the new price pressures hitting families and said next week’s budget would save “more than we spend”. “The uncertainty and volatility in the global economy mean there is an even greater premium on responsible fiscal management,” he said. “The budget is already more than $233bn better than we inherited because the government has found savings, kept a lid on spending growth and banked revenue upgrades. “In the upcoming budget, we’ll continue that record of responsible economic management by saving more than we spend and banking all upward revisions to revenue.” The RBA warned inflation could rocket higher if the Iran war dragged on, with more tightening of monetary policy required. Australia Institute senior economist Matt Grudnoff blasted the rate rise, saying the RBA had only heaped more pressure on “already stretched households”. “The RBA made the wrong decision,” he said. “Higher interest rates will do nothing to open the Strait of Hormuz. Higher interest rates cannot change the world price of oil and bring down fuel prices. “All this does is heap more pain on already stretched households. The only tool the RBA has to fight inflation is to change interest rates. But interest rates are ineffective at stopping inflation caused by supply shocks ... higher fuel costs and now a third interest rate increase this year is likely to impact economic growth and push unemployment higher.” KPMG chief economist Dr Brendan Rynne said the RBA’s ‘softly-softly’ post-Covid approach to taming inflation rather than the aggressive action taken by other Central Banks had backfired. “The consequence of this ‘novel’ approach is now coming home to roost, with Australia leading the next monetary policy tightening cycle globally in an attempt to temper demand and lower inflation,” he said. “The RBA is now realising that maintaining credibility in its inflation fight is more important than remaining fixated on full employment.” He said last month’s inflation surge was likely the ‘calm before the storm’ with more interest rate hikes to come. Nationwide News Pty Ltd. summary: The Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) has raised the cash rate by 0.25 percentage points to 4.35%, marking the third increase this year, as inflation pressures continue to rise due to the Iran War energy shock. RBA Governor Michele Bullock stated that Australians are now 'poorer' due to increased oil prices and warned of slowing economic growth and rising recession risks. The RBA's actions aim to temper inflation expectations, but further rate hikes may be necessary if government spending continues to exacerbate demand.
publication:
Aussies reject AI – but it’s not for the reason you think
theaustralian.com.au04 May 2026 20:30 GMT783 wordsJared Lynch

Chief executives of Australia’s biggest companies and Anthony Albanese want us all to use more artificial intelligence-powered tools – but there is a big problem.
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: RBA raises cash rate to 4.35% amid rising inflation
Governor Bullock states Australians are 'poorer' due to external shocks
Further rate hikes may occur if government spending increases demand
relevance: This news is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals as it impacts interest rates, inflation forecasts, and overall economic conditions that influence financial planning and risk assessment.
